message:
ci(release): make Windows pwsh smoke step fail on ft.exe non-zero exit (ft-uokgq)

2f25ea5d's Windows smoke step invoked ft.exe via `& $binary ...` but
didn't set $ErrorActionPreference or $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference.
GitHub Actions' default pwsh invocation doesn't set either, so a non-zero
exit from ft.exe only populated $LASTEXITCODE without terminating the
script — the final Write-Host would exit 0 and the whole step would
report success, silently shipping a Windows-only regression (panic at
startup, crash on --help, non-zero `ft doctor --json`).

The Unix step gets `set -eo pipefail` implicitly from Actions' default
bash args; pwsh has to opt in. Added at the top of the pwsh block:

    $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
    $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $true

`$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference` is PowerShell 7.3+; GHA
Windows runners ship pwsh 7.4+. With both set, any `& $binary ...` exit
≠ 0 now raises a terminating error and the step fails.

Defeats the fail-open that broke the intent of ft-ych74 / 2f25ea5d.

Agent: RusticMeadow

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ft-05hfm","title":"[LOW] pattern-pack loader + wa.send have no size/depth caps; hostile input can DoS via memory","description":"Found during /security-audit-for-saas pass #8 (pane 5, 2026-04-21).

## Summary
Two unrelated input-validation gaps surfaced while auditing CLI and
MCP argument handling:

- **F1 (MEDIUM):** `load_pack_from_file` at patterns.rs:978 reads
  the entire pattern-pack file into memory via
  `std::fs::read_to_string` with NO size cap, then hands the bytes
  to `serde_yaml::from_str` / `serde_json::from_str` / `toml::
  from_str` with NO depth cap. A hostile `.ft/patterns/huge.yaml`
  (or JSON / TOML) in a cloned repo can exhaust the allocator or
  stack-overflow the YAML parser on deeply-nested constructs.
- **F2 (LOW):** `WaSendTool::call` and `WaRulesTestTool::call` do
  not bound the length of `params.text` before dispatching it.
  An MCP client can submit a multi-gigabyte `text` field; the MCP
  layer will buffer it, policy-evaluate it, and try to send it
  through `wezterm cli send-text`.

## Evidence

### F1
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs:978
  ```rust
  fn load_pack_from_file(path: &str, root: Option<&Path>) -> Result<PatternPack> {
      ...
      let content = std::fs::read_to_string(&resolved)
          .map_err(|e| PatternError::PackNotFound(...))?;
      let pack = match ext.as_str() {
          "yaml" | "yml" => serde_yaml::from_str(&content)...,
          "json" => serde_json::from_str(&content)...,
          "toml" => toml::from_str(&content)...,
          ...
      };
  ```
  No size cap on `content`. No depth cap on the deserializer.
  `serde_yaml` does not guard against billion-laughs by default
  (anchors are resolved in the YAML 1.2 spec), and deeply-nested
  constructs can stack-blow before the typed Deserialize even
  starts. Same attack class as ft-xv561 (ReDoS via hostile
  pattern regex), different primitive.

### F2
- crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:1799
  schema declares `"text": { "type": "string" }` with no
  `maxLength`. The tool body does not validate `params.text.len()`
  before `injector.send_text(..., &params.text, ...)`.
- Same gap in `WaRulesTestTool` at :420.
- `WaSendTool` does pass `params.text` through `engine.
  redact_secrets(...)` for the audit summary at :1876, but the
  raw text still goes downstream to wezterm without a length
  bound.

## Impact
Severity: LOW-MEDIUM.

- F1 requires the attacker to land a file inside a workspace the
  user clones, then `ft watch` (or any pattern-engine user)
  inhales it. DoS on the local process, not a remote exploit.
- F2 requires an MCP client — stdio transport runs under the
  user's uid/gid, so the attacker already has process
  equivalence. The concern is operator-hygiene: an auto-MCP
  pipeline that doesn't bound-check outbound `text` could OOM
  the `ft watch` process.

## Fix direction

### F1 — pattern pack loader
1. Cap `content` size before deserialize. Pick a pragmatic limit
   — e.g. `MAX_PACK_BYTES: usize = 1 * 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB). A
   legitimate pattern pack is a handful of rules and text; 1 MiB
   is three orders of magnitude above reasonable. Reject with
   `PatternError::PackTooLarge(path, size)`.
2. Use `serde_yaml::Deserializer::from_str(content).depth_limit(32)`
   — serde_yaml exposes the builder hook; default stack budget
   grows linearly with nesting.
3. Propagate parse errors without embedding the raw content in
   diagnostics (already done via `PatternError::InvalidRule(format!(
   "invalid YAML pack: {e}"))`).

### F2 — wa.send text length
1. Add `MAX_SEND_TEXT_BYTES: usize = 1 * 1024 * 1024` (1 MiB) at
   the WaSendTool::call entry.
2. Reject with `MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS` and a hint that pane input
   of legitimate shape should never exceed this bound.
3. Mirror the cap in WaRulesTestTool.

## Non-findings verified in the same pass

- `pane_id` bounds on `ft robot send`: `pane_id: u64` via clap.
  No upper bound at the CLI layer. Downstream `wezterm.send_text`
  looks up the pane by id; a nonexistent id returns
  `WeztermError::PaneNotFound`. No injection vector — `pane_id`
  is decimal-encoded into `--pane-id <N>` arg of `wezterm cli
  send-text`, which cannot be reinterpreted as a shell
  metacharacter. Clean.
- `ft snapshot restore <id>` path traversal: `snapshot_id` is
  parsed via `resolve_checkpoint_id` at main.rs:8208 which
  accepts only the literal "latest" or an `i64`. No path, no
  filesystem lookup. Clean.
- clap `env()` fallback on any arg: grep across
  `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs` for `env(\"` returned zero
  hits. No argument pulls its value from an environment
  variable, so a process-env manipulation cannot bypass a
  missing CLI flag. Clean.

## Related
- ft-xv561 (070117a0) — same attack class (hostile pattern pack
  → DoS), different primitive (regex backtrack vs YAML parse).
- ft-kegvt (969b9e02) — same "restricted-input" family: user-
  supplied strings flowing into a trusted sink without a
  structural cap.

+{"id":"ft-080mv","title":"[review][LOW] ft-8ybme scan_pipeline fail-fast covers panic path but not recovery (flush-then-retry) path","description":"658b945a added an assert! at crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs:466-469 that panics when `process_chunk` is called with non-empty bytes after `should_flush` returned true. The regression test `chunked_process_chunk_panics_when_appending_after_flush_pending` at :940-950 exercises the PANIC path: overflow the buffer, assert should_flush=true, call process_chunk again, catch the panic via #[should_panic].

Not covered: the RECOVERY path. A well-behaved caller handles should_flush by calling flush_chunked (or equivalent) to drain the buffer, then continues process_chunk'ing. There is no test that verifies:

1. After should_flush=true, calling flush_chunked() resets should_flush back to false.
2. After a successful flush, a subsequent process_chunk does NOT panic and correctly resumes scanning.
3. Aho-Corasick LeftmostFirst invariant holds across the flush boundary — per MEMORY.md, the engine requires accumulate-then-batch-rescan at checkpoints. If flush resets trigger_data_buffer but the scan_state continues, a pattern that straddles the flush boundary could be missed OR double-counted depending on trigger scan semantics.

Fix path: add a positive test:

    #[test]
    fn chunked_process_chunk_resumes_after_flush() {
        let pipeline = ScanPipeline::default();
        let mut state = ChunkedPipelineState::new(64);

        pipeline.process_chunk(&[b'x'; 80], &mut state);
        assert!(state.should_flush());

        let flushed = pipeline.flush_chunked(&mut state);
        assert!(!state.should_flush(), "flush must reset the pending indicator");

        // Second chunk containing a trigger that would have been missed
        // if scan_state weren't preserved across the flush:
        let second = pipeline.process_chunk(b"error occurred", &mut state);
        assert!(second.trigger_counts.get(&TriggerCategory::Error).copied().unwrap_or(0) >= 1);
    }

If the recovery path is also flaky (e.g., scan_state doesn't survive flush, or Aho-Corasick batch rescan loses matches at the boundary) the test above would catch it.

Severity: LOW — panic path is tested and correct; positive path is an unverified assumption. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying 658b945a.

 {"id":"ft-0c8wp","title":"Correct README.md numerical claims: workspace crate count, vendored crate count, and module count","description":"# Goal
Fix the overstated numbers in README.md so the documentation matches reality. Documentation credibility matters — inflated numbers undermine trust in claims that ARE accurate.

# Background
The 2026-04-06 reality check found:
- README claims "120 workspace crates" → Actual: 63 workspace members in root Cargo.toml
- README claims "105 crates" from ex-WezTerm → Actual: 45 active vendored crates in frankenterm/ directory (95+ if counting legacy_wezterm/ checkpoint that isn't part of the workspace)
- README claims "482 modules" → Actual: 483 .rs files (off by 1, essentially correct)
- README claims "775k lines" → Actual: 793,698 lines (grew since README was written)
- README claims "45,000+ tests" → Actual: 46,633 (accurate, slightly understated)

# Changes Required
1. Update "120 workspace crates" to actual count. Options:
   a. "63 workspace crates" (strict workspace members)
   b. "63 workspace crates (plus 95+ legacy reference crates)" (honest with context)
2. Update vendored crate reference to "45 active vendored crates"
3. Update module count to 483 (or "480+" for durability)
4. Update line count to ~794k (or "790k+" for durability)
5. Keep test count as "45,000+" (still accurate)
6. Add a "last verified" date footnote for numerical claims

# Acceptance Criteria
1. Every numerical claim in README.md matches a verifiable count.
2. No claim is inflated. Rounding is always down or uses "N+" notation.
3. Badge at top of README matches actual numbers.
4. AGENTS.md workspace structure section is consistent with README.

# Implementation Notes
- The exact count will drift as development continues, so use "N+" notation for fast-moving numbers.
- The badge "Lines of Code" currently says "775k lines" — update to match.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":30,"created_at":"2026-04-06T18:55:20.592960Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-07T01:46:03.711999Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T01:46:03.711832Z","close_reason":"Updated README summary counts to verified current values","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["accuracy","docs","readme"]}

 {"id":"ft-0e179","title":"[mor:distributed-systems] backpressure drops lose per-pane attribution","description":"# Observation

`BackpressureMetrics::segments_dropped` (crates/frankenterm-core/src/backpressure.rs:224)
is a single `AtomicU64`. When red/black-tier capture shedding fires and
segments are dropped, operators see **how many** were lost but not
**from which pane**.

In an incident ("rate limit slipped past detection at 02:14"), the
on-call needs to determine whether the dropped segment interval
included the pane they're debugging. Today that's un-answerable — the
drops counter is scalar.

# Question

Should we shift `segments_dropped` to a per-pane `AtomicU64` (keyed by
pane_id) so a 3am operator can pivot from "27 segments dropped in the
last minute" to "23 of those were pane 4167 (claude-code), 4 were pane
4203 (codex)"? Memory cost is bounded (≤ 8 bytes × N_panes).

Adjacent thought: the gap marker emitted to storage (`gaps_emitted`)
presumably already carries pane identity; could we expose a thin
aggregate view over gap markers instead of teaching backpressure to
track per-pane, keeping the drop-path allocation-free?

# CAP stance

Current design: chose availability (keep writing, drop segments when
queue full) over consistency (refuse writes to preserve ordering). The
tradeoff is sound for a terminal observation platform — we want the
next byte more than the missed one. But per-pane attribution turns
lost observations into *known* lost observations, which is the
difference between a bug report and a silent false-negative.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:34:33.894245Z","created_by":"mor-review","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:43.786242Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:56:59.638045Z","close_reason":"Fixed in bd8a715e: BackpressureMetrics gains per-pane HashMap (dropped_by_pane: RwLock<HashMap<u64, AtomicU64>>) + 4-method read surface (total, per-pane, snapshot, distinct-pane count). RuntimeMetrics.backpressure: Arc<BackpressureMetrics> threads the metric to native-event drop sites in runtime.rs::emit_native_output_delta and handle_native_event. 6 regression tests including concurrent-drop stress. Changes bundled into the concurrent ft-fbee5 commit during a race; code is in main. native_events.rs EventDispatchOutcome::Backpressure drop site left unwired as a follow-up (no RuntimeMetrics reach from that dispatcher).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["distributed-systems","mor","observability"]}

 {"id":"ft-2xnop","title":"[MEDIUM] pty CommandBuilder serde proptests skip serialized unix umask state","description":"[MEDIUM] Recent commit c664dc39 (test: extend pty serde proptests) added frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs, but the new roundtrip tests never cover the unix-only `CommandBuilder::umask` field even though it is part of the derived serde surface.

Evidence:
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:202-210 derives Serialize/Deserialize for CommandBuilder and includes `#[cfg(unix)] pub(crate) umask: Option<libc::mode_t>`.
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:423-425 exposes `CommandBuilder::umask(...)` to mutate that serialized field.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:35-52 `build_command(...)` never sets umask.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:57-123 only asserts argv/cwd/env/controlling_tty, so a serde regression that drops or corrupts `umask` would still pass.

Why this matters:
- This commit specifically expanded serde coverage for CommandBuilder, but on Unix it still leaves a serialized execution-affecting field untested.
- `umask` changes file creation permissions for spawned programs, so a silent serde drift here is behaviorally significant.

Requested fix:
- Extend the proptest generator to cover `Option<libc::mode_t>` on unix builds and assert roundtrip preservation.
- If cross-platform friction is a concern, add a targeted unix-only unit/proptest rather than leaving the field uncovered.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:52:51.639844Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.030677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.029943Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-326s9","title":"Fix bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls test — config shape contradicts production logic","description":"# Pre-existing mTLS test failure

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 359 verification work. The test
`distributed::tests::bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls` at
`crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs:3805` fails reproducibly with:

    client bundle: MissingClientCaPath

## Reproduction
```
cargo test -p frankenterm-core --features distributed \
    --lib bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls -- --nocapture
```

## Root cause
The test constructs a `client_cfg` with `auth_mode =
DistributedAuthMode::Mtls` but without `client_ca_path` populated:

```rust
let mut client_cfg = DistributedConfig::default();
client_cfg.enabled = true;
client_cfg.auth_mode = DistributedAuthMode::Mtls;
client_cfg.tls.enabled = true;
client_cfg.tls.cert_path = Some(client_cert.path().display().to_string());
client_cfg.tls.key_path = Some(client_key.path().display().to_string());
// client_ca_path intentionally left as None.
```

Then it calls `build_tls_bundle(&client_cfg, Some(ca_cert.path()))`. Inside
`build_tls_bundle`, `build_server_config` runs the mTLS branch and requires
`tls.client_ca_path` (the cert used to verify INCOMING client certs). It is
None → `DistributedTlsError::MissingClientCaPath`.

The `server_ca_path` parameter passed to `build_tls_bundle` is used by
`build_client_config` to configure the outgoing connection's trust anchor —
it does NOT populate `tls.client_ca_path`.

## The tension
- `ft-xbnl0.2.4` tick 337 test `build_tls_bundle_rejects_mtls_without_client_ca_path`
  explicitly pins "Mtls auth without client_ca_path → `MissingClientCaPath`"
  as the intended production contract.
- The failing test expects the opposite (that this config shape succeeds).

## Options
1. Fix the test: set `client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path = Some(ca_cert.path().display().to_string())` alongside the existing cert/key.
2. Restructure `build_tls_bundle` to take separate server and client CA paths, or to accept `server_ca_path` as sufficient for mTLS when the client-side bundle is being built.
3. Deprecate the failing test if the config shape was only ever tentative.

## Attribution
- Authored: jemanuel on 2026-04-10 (commit 147b22176).
- Never fixed or touched since.
- Observed failing on 2026-04-18 during ft-xbnl0.2.4 verification sweep.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The bead-scoped check script (`scripts/check_ft_xbnl0_2_4.sh`) uses `--lib tls_`
filter which does not catch this test (the name has `mtls` with no `tls_`
substring). All 73 tests in that filter pass. The failure surfaces only on
broader `--lib distributed::tests::` filter.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.2`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:36.191788Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047438Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047196Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 362 (commit c799aad4): populated client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path in the test, matching the tick-337 positive-guard contract. Broad distributed::tests:: filter now 154/154 ok (was 153/154).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mtls","pre-existing","test"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ft-326s9","depends_on_id":"ft-xbnl0.2.4","type":"related","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:05:03.143481Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"ft-3b0gh","title":"[review][LOW] build_trigger_automaton panic message drops the underlying aho-corasick error","description":"commit 4219e912 added crates/frankenterm-core/src/pattern_trigger.rs:272-276:

    Some(
        builder
            .build(patterns)
            .expect("non-empty trigger pattern sets must compile"),
    )

The .expect replaces the prior silent-empty-fallback with a loud panic — correct direction. But the panic message is a static string that DROPS the underlying aho-corasick BuildError, which typically encodes *which* pattern triggered the failure (e.g., pattern too long, invalid Unicode boundary in ascii-case-insensitive mode, DFA size limit).

A user hitting this panic in production sees:

    thread 'main' panicked at 'non-empty trigger pattern sets must compile'

…with no indication of *which* rule's pattern broke. Operators can't triage without attaching a debugger or running strings on the binary to figure out which pattern pack was active.

Fix (one-line change):

    .unwrap_or_else(|err| panic!("non-empty trigger pattern sets must compile: {err}"))

OR use .map_err + .expect:

    .map_err(|err| format!("non-empty trigger pattern sets must compile: {err}"))
    .expect(...)

Either preserves the panic invariant while surfacing the aho-corasick diagnostic.

Minor concern, not a correctness bug. Discovered during reviewer audit round 9, 2026-04-23 while verifying ft-lfnwn fix.

 {"id":"ft-4o5mb","title":"[conformance] wa.search / wa.get_text / wa.send / wa.wait_for lack schema+envelope golden roundtrip","description":"# Gap

`crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs` pins envelope + schema conformance for exactly ONE tool: `wa.rules_list` (tests at lines 237-294, 313-346). `conformance_mcp_manifest.rs` pins the MANIFEST (tool schemas, additionalProperties=false, resource templates) but does NOT exercise the per-tool request → response envelope roundtrip.

`grep -oE '"wa\.[a-z_]+"' crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance.rs | sort -u` → only `"wa.rules_list"`.

Out of 29 advertised `wa.*` tools in `tests/fixtures/mcp_manifest.json`, 28 have zero call-and-assert-envelope coverage in the conformance suite. The four core operator-facing tools are:

* `wa.search` — JSON envelope shape, hit schema, TOON parity, pane_filter/since_filter/until_filter/mode/metrics fields
* `wa.get_text` — pane_text map shape (ok/error variants), truncated + truncation_info edges
* `wa.send` — sender/response envelope (distinct from get_text), error codes for denied sends
* `wa.wait_for` — matched/elapsed_ms/polls/is_regex fields, timeout response envelope, rule_id match path

## Shape of fix

Mirror the `mcp_conformance_rules_list_*` test pattern (lines 237-294 in mcp_conformance.rs):

1. spawn_client() → call_tool("wa.search", …)
2. parse_tool_envelope() → assert_success_envelope_shape() → assert_*_data_matches_documented_schema()
3. Also pin: invalid-format rejection, invalid-args hint text, TOON parity where applicable.

Existing helpers (`assert_object_matches_documented_schema`, `assert_common_envelope_fields`, `assert_success_envelope_shape`, `assert_invalid_args_envelope_shape`) already generalise — the new tests should reuse them verbatim.

## Why this matters

The MCP manifest conformance is the FREEZE on the advertised schema. Without per-tool envelope tests, a refactor can pass `conformance_mcp_manifest` (schema still looks right) while silently returning a non-conforming response body — e.g. field renamed in `ScannerHit` → clients break but tests stay green. ft-bs0ec froze this for `agent_inventory`; the four core operator tools deserve the same treatment.

## Scope guard

One new test file `mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs` (or additions to `mcp_conformance.rs`) with 4× (success + invalid-args) = 8 tests. ~250 LOC. No production changes.

## Explicitly out of scope

Mission / tx / events / cass families (separate bead if needed). Manifest tests (already covered).  {"id":"ft-4qom2","title":"[SEV] tmux cmd_queue cap only enforced at pop site; unbounded growth when state != Idle","description":"frankenterm/mux/src/tmux.rs TmuxDomainState.cmd_queue has CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH=50_000 enforced only inside send_next_command at line 289 via cmd_queue.drain(..excess). send_next_command early-returns at line 286-288 when state != Idle, so the drain-oldest logic never runs if tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse.

Four push sites append to cmd_queue with NO cap check:
- line 196: Event::LayoutChange -> ListAllPanes
- line 223: Event::SessionChanged -> ListCommands
- line 234: Event::WindowAdd -> ListAllWindows
- line 375: split_pane (user split) -> SplitPane

Concrete interleaving: (1) tmux sends a command, enters State::WaitingForResponse; (2) tmux server is slow/hung (real-world scenario: remote tmux on lagging SSH link, tmux server overloaded, Event::Guarded response delayed); (3) GUI user resizes window repeatedly -> stream of Event::LayoutChange -> each push_back at line 196 runs without cap check; (4) send_next_command early-returns because state != Idle. Queue grows unbounded -> memory leak, eventual OOM.

The CMD_QUEUE_MAX_DEPTH comment at line 32-33 explicitly advertises 'prevent unbounded memory growth during event storms' as a safety invariant. That invariant is currently only upheld at the pop site, where it can't run under the exact condition (stuck tmux) that event storms exploit.

Fix: extract the cap-enforcement logic into a push_command_capped helper that applies the drain-oldest + warn/error logging at every push site. The helper runs under the cmd_queue lock (just acquired by the caller), so it's atomic with the push and has no additional locking cost.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T00:17:26.601099Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.160715Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T00:21:03.158111Z","close_reason":"Completed: added push_command_capped() helper and routed all 4 production push sites (LayoutChange/SessionChanged/WindowAdd/split_pane) through it. The hard cap is now enforced at push time, not only at pop time — closing the unbounded-growth window when tmux is stuck in State::WaitingForResponse. cargo check passes clean. Commit 1055cffc.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-4z7vh","title":"RobotCommands::Workflow builds PolicyEngine without require_prompt_active/command_gate/rules — bypasses operator safety config","description":"AUTH BYPASS (robot mode mutations, Severity: Medium).

crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs has 10 PolicyEngine::new call sites. 8 of them wire require_prompt_active from config + .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()). Two do NOT, both under RobotCommands::Workflow:

Site 1 (main.rs:19831, RobotCommands::Workflow::Run):

  let policy_engine = PolicyEngine::new(
      config.safety.rate_limit_per_pane,
      config.safety.rate_limit_global,
      false, // Don't require prompt active for robot mode
  )
  .with_tuning(&config.tuning);

No command_gate, no policy_rules, and require_prompt_active hardcoded false. This engine is fed into CxPolicyInjector → WorkflowRunner; any workflow step that calls send_text (e.g., handle_compaction sending '/compact', handle_usage_limit sending '/refresh') bypasses:
  - the operator's safety.require_prompt_active=true policy,
  - every [[safety.rules]] custom gate,
  - the [safety.command_gate] allow/deny list.

Site 2 (main.rs:20511, RobotCommands::Workflow::Abort): identical construction. abort_execution_with_cx runs compensation steps through the same injector; any send_text in a compensation path bypasses the same three surfaces.

Compare the correctly-gated paths:
  - RobotCommands::Send at main.rs:17652 uses config.safety.require_prompt_active AND .with_command_gate_config + .with_policy_rules.
  - Non-robot workflow invocations at main.rs:21361, 21533, 25051, 27347 all wire the full policy surface.

NOT a duplicate of ft-48ah6 (read-only policy): this bead is specifically about the write/mutation surface (workflow's send_text / command-bearing steps). ft-48ah6 covered the read/search side.

Fix: replace the hardcoded 'false' with config.safety.require_prompt_active at both sites, and chain .with_command_gate_config(config.safety.command_gate.clone()) + .with_policy_rules(config.safety.rules.clone()) before passing the engine to PolicyGatedInjector. Rate-limit args are already correct.

Regression test: a send inside a robot-invoked workflow must be DENIED when config.safety.require_prompt_active=true AND no prompt is active, matching the direct-send gate behavior. Currently it is allowed.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane5","created_at":"2026-04-23T06:46:59.925741Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084786Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T07:19:54.084480Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 834b59f2.  {"id":"ft-55l4f","title":"[review][MEDIUM] wa_wait_for.json golden freezes polls:1 — may flake under high-concurrency CI load","description":"In crates/frankenterm-core/tests/golden_robot_envelope/wa_wait_for.json (landed in ecae6caa):

    "polls": 1

The canonicalize fn in crates/frankenterm-core/tests/mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs only zeroes `now | elapsed_ms | captured_at | *_ms | score | semantic_score`. The `polls` field is not canonicalized.

Under load, this can flake:
- Each wa.wait_for poll spawns a fresh python3 subprocess (fake-wezterm.py) via tokio::Command in crates/frankenterm-core/src/wezterm.rs:2142-2155.
- On first-poll cache-cold with the swarm running 20+ agents hammering CPU/disk, the subprocess spawn + file read can be slow enough that the poll scheduler issues a second poll before the first returns matching text (build ready matches ready$ immediately on content).
- polls jumps to 2+, golden is polls==1, test fails.

Repro likelihood: low under bench, moderate-to-high under concurrent cargo test + rch + other agent builds hammering I/O.

Fix options (pick one):
1. Canonicalize `polls` to 0 in canonicalize() alongside elapsed_ms/now/captured_at. Pro: stable golden. Con: loses a genuine signal — golden no longer catches regressions where wait_for suddenly needs 10 polls for a match it used to catch on the first try.
2. Split `polls` out of the golden capture: keep it in success_envelope but canonicalize to "polls >= 1" assertion in assert_wait_for_success_data (already there at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:~590 — `polls >= 1`), then canonicalize polls to 0 so the golden only sees the structural presence.
3. Accept the flake risk and document that re-running tests clears transient failures.

Option 2 is cleanest: the semantic "polls >= 1" invariant stays enforced; the byte-stable golden covers structure.

Severity: MEDIUM. Not a correctness bug; CI flake risk. Discovered during reviewer audit round 7, 2026-04-23 when verifying ecae6caa (ft-qyw1z+ft-4o5mb CRITICAL fix).

Semantic invariant polls>=1 remains enforced in assert_wait_for_success_data at mcp_conformance_core_tools.rs:474-477 (runs against the live envelope at :733, BEFORE canonicalization). canonicalize() at :480 now zeroes polls alongside now/elapsed_ms/captured_at so the byte-stable golden only freezes structural presence, not scheduler-timing-dependent count. wa_wait_for.json updated to polls:0 to match.  {"id":"ft-6269x","title":"[reality-gap] ft snapshot restore Unix-only status is not disclosed in README session-persistence matrix","description":"# Claim (README)

Undisclosed: `ft snapshot restore` has the *same* cfg gate.

# Reality (code)

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:27100` (ft restart — disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft restart is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

`crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs:39715` (ft snapshot restore — NOT disclosed):

    eprintln!("ft snapshot restore is currently supported only on Unix platforms.");

Same gate, same failure mode. README line 128 specifically mentions
`ft restart` as the Unix-only surface but omits `ft snapshot restore`,
which is also mentioned in the *same* matrix row ("Snapshots, session
inspection, **restore**, and `ft session doctor` are implemented").

A Windows operator reading the matrix is told "restore is
implemented" (true, on Unix) without the cross-platform caveat.

Operator on Windows runs `ft snapshot restore ...` → eprintln hits
→ exit 1. Workaround unknown.

Update README.md:128 to say:

    Snapshots and session inspection are implemented cross-platform;
    `ft restart` and `ft snapshot restore` are currently Unix-only.

One-line doc change. Alternative: fix the cfg gate. The session
persistence code under `#[cfg(unix)]` likely depends on real Unix
signals / fork semantics — porting is non-trivial. Docs-first is
the pragmatic path.

ft-2guux previously flagged G2 "`ft restart` Unix-only not in
support matrix" and was closed 2026-04-21 with a README.md:128

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). +{"id":"ft-g3z31","title":"[review][LOW] ft-ojgmo lacks regression test for pre-cancel fast-path in EventSubscriber::recv_cx","description":"fa1fbffe added two cancel-detection paths to EventSubscriber::recv_cx at crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:789-817:

1. Pre-cancel fast-path at :793:
       if cx.is_cancel_requested() { return Err(RecvError::Cancelled); }
2. Mid-flight cancel watcher at :798-806 (select race with 50ms polling).

The new regression test `event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_mid_flight_cancel` at :1573-1601 exercises the MID-FLIGHT path only — cancel is signalled 100ms AFTER recv_cx is entered. The PRE-CANCEL fast-path (cx already cancelled when recv_cx is called) is untested at the EventSubscriber layer.

Evidence: search for calls to EventSubscriber::recv_cx with a pre-cancelled cx in the test suite — none exist. The runtime_compat.rs test at :6189 tests pre-cancel on the lower-level broadcast_recv_with_cx primitive, but not the EventSubscriber wrapper that is the public API.

Why it matters: the pre-cancel shortcut is one line, but it was the difference between a hot loop of polling wakeups and immediate return. A refactor that moves the cancel check inside the select! block (losing the shortcut) would regress the behavior silently — the mid-flight test would still pass because the 50ms poll would eventually fire.

Fix: add a sibling test:

    #[test]
    fn event_subscriber_recv_cx_surfaces_pre_cancel_immediately() {
        run_async_test(async {
            let cx = crate::cx::Cx::for_testing();
            cx.cancel_with(crate::outcome::CancelKind::User, Some("pre-cancel"));
            let bus = EventBus::new(8);
            let mut sub = bus.subscribe();
            let started = Instant::now();
            let result = sub.recv_cx(&cx).await;
            assert!(matches!(result, Err(RecvError::Cancelled)));
            assert!(started.elapsed() < Duration::from_millis(5),
                "pre-cancel must return immediately, took {:?}", started.elapsed());
        });
    }

A <5ms assertion distinguishes the shortcut path from the 50ms poll path.

Severity: LOW — not a correctness bug, just a coverage gap. Discovered during reviewer audit round 11, 2026-04-23 while verifying fa1fbffe.

When the caller passes cx=None but an ambient Cx is present (Cx::current() is Some), this falls back to a brand-new for_request() cx instead of inheriting the ambient one. Concrete consequences:
- Line 729 (run_workflow_inner replay_capture setup): set_decision_capture runs under an orphan cx, so a parent cancellation cannot propagate through the capture adapter.
- Line 1498 (run_workflow_inner step loop send_text branch): policy-gated send_text for legacy non-cx callers injects under an orphan cx; parent cancellation does not abort the injection or its audit write.
- Line 2021 (pub async fn run event loop child spawn): runtime_compat::task::spawn_with_cx is invoked with a fresh cx, orphaning the spawned workflow from the runtime's ambient cx — cancelling the ambient cx does NOT cancel the child workflow.

Real non-test callers hit these paths: mcp_tools.rs:2145 calls runner.run_workflow(...) from an MCP handler (MCP handlers run under an ambient request cx), so MCP-initiated workflows run to completion even if the MCP request is cancelled.  {"id":"ft-tzwuw","title":"mcp_tools ca.search/ca.view/ca.status accept timeout_secs=0, fire immediate-timeout","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` cass tools (ca.search line
590, ca.view line 683, ca.status line 763) all pass
\`params.timeout_secs\` directly to \`cass_client_with_timeout()\` without
validating that it's >= 1.

The JSON-Schema declarations for these tools advertise
\`"timeout_secs": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 1, "maximum": 600,
"default": 15 }\` (lines 526, 636, 726), but \`serde_json::from_value\`
does not enforce JSON-Schema bounds — it happily deserializes \`0\` as
a valid u64.

## Concrete failure path

1. MCP client (or any caller that doesn't pre-validate against the
   schema) sends \`{"query": "x", "timeout_secs": 0}\` to ca.search.
2. \`serde_json::from_value\` accepts the 0 → \`params.timeout_secs = 0\`.
3. \`cass_client_with_timeout(0)\` constructs a CassClient with
   \`timeout = Duration::from_secs(0)\` (cass.rs:781-784).
4. \`run_with_cx\` (cass.rs:961) calls
   \`timeout_with_cx(cx, Duration::from_secs(0), cmd.output()).await\`
   — a zero-duration timeout fires immediately, before \`cmd.output()\`
   has any chance to execute.
5. Returns \`CassError::Timeout { timeout_secs: 0 }\` to the MCP client
   on EVERY call. The client sees a confusing "cass timeout (0 secs)"
   error and may not realize the issue is its own \`timeout_secs: 0\`
   parameter rather than a slow cass binary.

Same defect shape as ft-t62hq (already fixed for wa.wait_for and
wa.send tools at lines 1149, 1873) — three sibling cass tools were
missed in that sweep.

## Severity rationale

- Silent UX failure: caller's request always times out without ever
  invoking the underlying tool.
- Reachable via any client that doesn't validate against the
  advertised schema (in practice: most language SDKs that just pass
  through user-supplied JSON, plus any test fixture that omits
  timeout_secs default-checking).
- Not a crash, not data corruption.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-t62hq guard at the top of each \`call(\)\` for the three
cass tools:

\`\`\`rust
if params.timeout_secs == 0 {
    let envelope = McpEnvelope::<()>::error(
        MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS,
        "timeout_secs must be >= 1 (got 0)".to_string(),
        Some(
            "The ca.<tool> schema declares timeout_secs with \\
             minimum: 1; omit the field to use the default (15)."
                .to_string(),
        ),
        elapsed_ms(start),
    );
    return envelope_to_content(envelope);
}
\`\`\`

## Regression tests

Three integration-style tests at the params layer:
- \`ca_search_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_view_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`
- \`ca_status_rejects_zero_timeout_secs\`

Each invokes the tool with \`timeout_secs: 0\` and asserts the response
is an Error envelope with \`code = MCP_ERR_INVALID_ARGS\` and the
"timeout_secs must be >= 1" message — proving the guard fires before
\`cass_client_with_timeout\` is ever called.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs\` only. No schema changes —
the schemas already declare \`minimum: 1\`. No changes to \`cass.rs\` —
\`with_timeout_secs(0)\` is a documented "instant timeout" primitive

-{"id":"ft-uokgq","title":"[review][MEDIUM] release.yml Windows smoke test silently swallows native command failures","description":"commit 2f25ea5d added a Windows smoke test step using `shell: pwsh` at .github/workflows/release.yml:82-96. The script invokes the ft.exe binary via the PowerShell call operator (`& $binary --version`), but does NOT configure PowerShell to fail on native command errors:

    - name: Smoke test binary (Windows)
      if: runner.os == 'Windows'
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        $binary = "target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/${{ matrix.artifact }}"
        Write-Host "=== ft --version ==="
        & $binary --version
        ...
        & $binary doctor --json
        Write-Host "=== All smoke tests passed ==="

GitHub Actions default pwsh invocation is `pwsh -command ". '{0}'"` — no `-ErrorActionPreference Stop`, no `$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference`. When a native command like `ft doctor --json` exits non-zero:
- PowerShell sets `$LASTEXITCODE` but does NOT throw.
- The script continues past the failing command.
- The last statement is `Write-Host '=== All smoke tests passed ==='` which exits 0.
- The step's final exit code is 0 → GitHub Actions reports success.

Effect: a Windows-only regression where ft.exe panics at startup, crashes on --help, or exits non-zero from doctor would ship to release WITHOUT the workflow catching it. The whole point of the ft-ych74 fix was to close this gap for Windows — and this silently defeats it.

The Unix counterpart at :68-80 (`shell: bash` default) uses GitHub Actions' default args `bash --noprofile --norc -eo pipefail {0}` → `set -e` is implicit, any non-zero exit aborts the step immediately.

Fix (add at top of pwsh script block):

    $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
    $PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference = $true

`$PSNativeCommandUseErrorActionPreference` (PowerShell 7.3+) makes native command failures throw terminating errors under $ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'. pwsh on ubuntu-latest / windows-latest is 7.4+, so this is safe to enable.

Alternative: check `$LASTEXITCODE` after each `& $binary ...` call and `throw` explicitly.

Severity: MEDIUM. Defeats the intent of 2f25ea5d (which closed my earlier bead ft-ych74). Discovered during reviewer audit round 9, 2026-04-23.

 {"id":"ft-up6u2","title":"[security] mission + tx contract loaders lack size cap — OOM vector for hostile repo .ft/mission/*.json","description":"# Bug

`mcp_load_mission_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:257) and
`mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path` (mcp_missions.rs:141) both
use bare `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` with no stat-before-read
size guard.

Pattern-pack loader has this exact guard (ft-05hfm, MAX_PACK_BYTES =
16 MiB). Mission files under `.ft/mission/*.json` have the same
trust property as patterns under `.ft/patterns/*.{yaml,json,toml}` —
a hostile repo can ship them, and they're loaded at MCP tool
invocation time.

# Attack

1. Hostile repo ships `.ft/mission/active.json` = 5 GiB of garbage.
2. Operator (or agent via wa.mission_state MCP) opens the project.
3. `wa.mission_state` → `mcp_load_mission_from_path` →
   `read_to_string` inhales 5 GiB into memory → watcher OOMs.

Same attack for `.ft/mission/tx-active.json` via wa.tx_show / wa.tx_run.

`resolve_workspace_scoped_path` (mcp_missions.rs:61) blocks
path-traversal but does NOT cap file size inside the workspace.

# Fix

Add `MAX_MISSION_BYTES` + `MAX_TX_CONTRACT_BYTES` constants (16 MiB
each, matching MAX_PACK_BYTES) with a stat-before-read check + a
second-line check after read, mirroring the pattern-pack loader
structure.

Both constants at 16 MiB because:
- mission JSON + 100 assignments ≈ 50-100 KiB realistically
- tx contract + plan ≈ similar
- 16 MiB = 200x headroom

# Tests

Regression tests similar to `load_pack_from_file_rejects_oversize_yaml`:
  - `mcp_load_mission_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`
  - `mcp_load_mission_tx_contract_from_path_rejects_oversize_json`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:25:19.149935Z","created_by":"pane3-mission-cap","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408462Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:45:21.408112Z","close_reason":"Landed in 3e2ae80c.  {"id":"ft-v0339","title":"[MEDIUM] integration_mux_checkpoint_topology post-rollback check only compares pane-id set, not tree shape","description":"Commit 30172171 fixed ft-olq2e by routing the post-rollback check through server.registry().snapshot() instead of the vacuous re-deserialization of initial_topo_json. However the new primary assertion at integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs still only compares PANE IDs:

  post_rollback_pane_ids_sorted == baseline_pane_ids

TopologySnapshot (crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_topology.rs) is a tree of windows → tabs → panes, with per-window position/size/title and per-tab pane layout metadata. The registry snapshot, filtered to LifecycleEntityKind::Pane, flattens all of that to a bag of pane u64 ids.

Consequence: a RemoteRequest::Rollback that restores the correct set of pane entities but (a) reparents them under the wrong tab, (b) drops a window or tab node, (c) corrupts the split tree under a tab, or (d) restores panes in the wrong order will still pass the assertion. Rollback tree-shape correctness is therefore still not covered by this test — the bead it closes was motivated precisely by false confidence in rollback behavior.

Expected fix:
- Reconstruct a post-rollback TopologySnapshot from the live server (registry + whatever stores tab/window edges) and compare it field-by-field against the pre-expansion baseline — at least window ids + tab ids + pane ids grouped by tab.
- Keep the pane-id-set assertion as a weaker redundant guard.

Regression risk: medium. The test name promises topology validation; reviewers relying on this bead's closure will assume tree-structure regressions are caught when in fact only pane existence is.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:02:21.495704Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017561Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T08:19:22.017321Z","close_reason":"Strengthened rollback validation to require exact TopologySnapshot restoration, not just pane-id/fingerprint agreement","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["mux","review","session-persistence","test-gap"]}
